1: ʾaḥar haddᵉḇārîm hāʾēlleh giḏdal hammelek ʾăḥašwērōš ʾet-hāmān ben-hammaḏāṯāʾ hāʾăgāgî: 'King Ahasuerus promoted Haman the son of Hammedatha the Agagite.' Hāmān is an Elamite or Persian name; Hammaḏāṯāʾ is Persian (Hamdātha = gift of Haoma, the sacred plant). Hāʾăgāgî (the Agagite) is the genealogically explosive epithet: Agag was the Amalekite king Saul spared in violation of YHWH's command (1 Samuel 15:8-9), resulting in Saul's rejection. The Agagite identification frames the narrative as renewal of the Saul-Agag conflict: Mordecai the Benjaminite (Saul's tribe and ancestral line) vs. Haman the Agagite (descendant of the king Saul should have killed). Wᵉyāśem ʾet-kissᵉʾô mēʿal kol-haśśārîm: 'set his seat above all princes' — Haman's elevation breeds the pride that a single non-bowing man will wound fatally.
2: wᵉḵol-ʿaḇdê hammelek ʾăšer bᵉšaʿar hammelek kōrᵉʿîm ûmištaḥăwîm lᵉhāmān kî-ḵēn ṣiwwāh lô hammelek: 'all the king's servants bowed and prostrated themselves — for so the king had commanded.' Kāraʿ (to kneel/bow) and hištaḥwāh (to prostrate) are the two terms for full obeisance. The prostration was not voluntary deference but royal command. Ûmorḏᵉḵay lōʾ yikraʿ wᵉlōʾ yištaḥăweh: 'but Mordecai would not kneel or bow.' The categorical refusal — stated twice in the negative — is the narrative's pivot. The reason (given in Esther 3:4: because he is a Jew) frames the refusal as religious conscience overriding imperial command — the classic diaspora dilemma, paralleled in Dan 3 (refusing to bow to the statue) and Dan 6 (refusing to stop praying). For a Jew, obeisance to a human being could compromise the exclusive worship due YHWH; the specific Agagite identity may have made prostration additionally impossible.
3: wayyōʾmᵉrû ʿaḇdê hammelek maddûaʿ ʾattāh ʿôḇēr ʾēt miṣwat hammelek: 'the king's servants said, why do you violate the king's command?' The interrogation by colleagues frames the issue as royal disobedience rather than religious principle — they use the language of legal violation (ʿāḇar ʾet miṣwat hammelek = transgress the king's command). Mordecai's answer (Esther 3:4) appeals to his Jewish identity. The collision between royal command and religious conscience is the recurring post-exilic dilemma (Dan 3, 6; Acts 4:19; Esther 5:29). The colleagues' questioning before reporting to Haman shows they are testing whether the refusal is deliberate or accidental — ensuring the subsequent conflict is fully informed and that Mordecai's principled stance is unambiguous.
4: wayhî kᵉʾomrām ʾēlāyw yôm wāyôm wᵉlōʾ šāmaʿ ʾălêhem: 'and when they had been speaking to him day after day (yôm wāyôm) and he would not listen.' The sustained daily pressure establishes Mordecai's refusal as consistent, principled stance — not a one-time oversight or misunderstanding. Wayyaggîḏû lᵉhāmān lirʾôt hayya'amᵉḏû diḇrê morḏᵉḵay: 'they reported to Haman to see whether Mordecai's behavior would be tolerated.' The colleagues are not merely informers but catalysts — they want the question resolved. Kî-higgîḏ lāhem ʾăšer-hûʾ yᵉhûḏî: 'for he had told them that he was a Jew' — Mordecai's own disclosure of his Jewish identity as the reason for refusal brings the latent Jew-Agagite conflict into the open where it must be resolved.
5: wayyarʾ hāmān kî-ʾên morḏᵉḵay kōrēaʿ ûmištaḥăweh lô wayyimmālēʾ hāmān ḥēmāh: 'when Haman saw that Mordecai would not kneel or bow, Haman was filled with wrath (ḥēmāh).' Ḥēmāh is the strongest Hebrew anger word — burning rage demanding violent action. The same word is used for YHWH's anger in prophetic contexts (Isaiah 63:3; Jeremiah 4:4; Ezekiel 8:18). Haman's disproportionate response — a single man's refusal produces genocidal fury — is characterologically diagnostic: he is constitutionally incapable of tolerating any challenge to his supremacy, however minor. The most powerful official in the Persian empire is undone by the indifference of a single gate-official — a portrait of the psychological fragility that lies beneath authoritarian power.
6: wayyiḇez bᵉʿênāyw lišlōaḥ yaḏ bᵉmorḏᵉḵay lᵉḇaḏô kî-higgîḏû lô ʾet-ʿam morḏᵉḵay: 'he disdained to lay hands on Mordecai alone, for they had told him Mordecai's people.' Bāzāh (to despise, disdain) — the same contempt-vocabulary used in Esther 1:17 (women despising their husbands). Wayyᵉḇaqšēš hāmān lᵉhašmîḏ ʾet-kol-hayyᵉhûḏîm: 'Haman sought to destroy all the Jews throughout the whole kingdom.' The logic of ethnic genocide: since the offender is representative of a people, the whole people becomes the target. This is the ideological structure of antisemitism — the individual Jew's behavior is taken as representative of Jewish collective threat, and the elimination of the individual is insufficient; the whole people must be eliminated. The personal grievance scales instantaneously to communal annihilation.
7: baḥōḏeš hārîʾšôn hûʾ-ḥōḏeš nîsān hippîl pûr hûʾ haggôrāl: 'in the first month, Nisan, they cast Pur — that is, the lot.' Pûr is a Persian/Akkadian loanword (pūru = lot, stone used in divination) — the narrator explains it equals Hebrew gôrāl (lot), anticipating the naming of the festival of Pûrîm (Esther 9:26-28). The lot was cast through all twelve months to find the most auspicious day — it fell on the twelfth month, Adar, giving eleven months before the decree's execution. This providential timing (the lot that Haman casts as a tool of murder gives his intended victims eleven months to respond) is the book's most ironic reversal: the pagan instrument of divination becomes the means by which divine timing creates the space for salvation.
8: yešnô ʿam-ʾeḥāḏ mᵉp̄uzzār ûmᵉp̄ōrāḏ bên hāʿammîm bᵉḵol-mᵉḏînôt malḵûteḵā: 'there is a certain people scattered and dispersed (mᵉp̄uzzār ûmᵉp̄ōrāḏ) throughout all the provinces of your kingdom.' The two participles emphasize the diaspora condition: dispersed widely, separated individually. Haman frames Jewish distinctiveness as imperial danger: their laws differ from every people (wᵉdātêhem šōnôt mikkol-ʿām), and they do not keep the king's laws. This is the perennial antisemitic accusation of dual loyalty — Jews have their own law that supersedes imperial law. The charge is not wholly inaccurate (Mordecai's refusal is an instance of Jewish law taking precedence) but is weaponized to justify genocide rather than to find accommodation.
9: waʾăšerāh ʿăśārāh kikkār-kesef ʿal-yᵉdê ʿōśê hamᵉlāʾḵāh lᵉhāḇîʾ ʾel-ginnᵉzê hammelek: 'and I will pay ten thousand talents of silver into the king's treasury.' Ten thousand talents = approximately 375 metric tons of silver — more than the Persian empire's annual tribute by some estimates (Herodotus gives total satrapal tribute at c. 7,740 Euboic talents). The offer may be rhetorical or reflect Haman's genuine extraordinary wealth. The king's response (Esther 3:11: 'the silver is yours') suggests either he trusted Haman to fund it or the offer was a political formality. In any case, the transaction reduces an entire ethnic community to a commercial exchange: their lives are purchased as a court favor.
10: wayyāsar hammelek ʾet-tabbaʿtô mēʿal yāḏô wayyittᵉnāh lᵉhāmān ben-hammaḏāṯāʾ hāʾăgāgî ṣōrēr hayyᵉhûḏîm: 'the king removed his signet ring (tabbaʿat) and gave it to Haman the Agagite, enemy (ṣōrēr) of the Jews.' Tabbaʿat (signet ring) — the instrument of royal authentication; documents sealed with the king's ring had royal authority. The transfer delegates power: Haman may now draft and seal decrees in the king's name. The narrator inserts the epithet ṣōrēr hayyᵉhûḏîm (adversary of the Jews) — from ṣārar (to press, show hostility), cognate with śāṭān (adversary). The king gives the ring without apparent inquiry into the target people — the casual disposal of a nation for court politics exposes the callousness of unchecked imperial power toward subject peoples.
11: wayyōʾmer hammelek lᵉhāmān hakkesef nātûn lāḵ wᵉhāʿām laʿăśôt bô kattôḇ bᵉʿênêḵā: 'the silver is given to you, and the people — to do with them as seems good to you.' The double gift (money and people) reduces an entire ethnic community to disposable property. The king's response is chillingly casual: no inquiry about which people, no examination of the charges, no legal process. Kattôḇ bᵉʿênêḵā (as seems good to you) — unconstrained discretion. The phrase echoes the Judges refrain (hayyāšār bᵉʿênāyw = what was right in his own eyes, Judges 21:25): where Judges deplores the chaos of moral anarchy, Esther deplores the chaos of imperial moral abdication — both are conditions where human sovereignty, unaccountable to divine law, produces catastrophe.
12: wayyiqqārᵉʾû sōp̄ᵉrê hammelek baḥōḏeš hārîʾšôn bišlōšāh ʿāśār yôm bô: 'the king's scribes were summoned on the thirteenth day of the first month.' The thirteenth of Nisan is the day before Passover — the irony is pointed: the day before Israel's great liberation festival, the decree for Israel's annihilation is written. Wayṯikkāṯēḇ kᵉḵol-ʾăšer ṣiwwāh hāmān: 'it was written exactly as Haman commanded.' The administrative machinery — scribes, satraps, governors, multiple scripts and languages — serves the genocide decree with the same efficiency it served the Cyrus liberation decree (Ezra 1:1-4). The bureaucratic apparatus is neutral; what it produces depends entirely on who controls it.
13: wᵉniślaḥ sᵉp̄ārîm bᵉyaḏ hārāṣîm ʾel-kol-mᵉḏînôt hammelek lᵉhašmîḏ lahărōg ûlᵉʾabbēd ʾet-kol-hayyᵉhûḏîm: 'letters were sent ordering the destruction (lᵉhašmîḏ), killing (lahărōg), and annihilation (lᵉʾabbēd) of all the Jews.' The triple destruction formula intensifies with each verb — eliminate, kill, destroy. Minnàʿar wᵉʿaḏ-zāqēn ṭap̄ wᵉnāšîm bᵉyôm ʾeḥāḏ: 'from young to old, children and women, in one day' — the comprehensiveness covers every demographic. Bišᵉlōšāh ʿāśār lᵉḥōḏeš šᵉnêm-ʿāśār hûʾ-ḥōḏeš ʾăḏār: 'on the thirteenth of the twelfth month, Adar' — the lot-determined date. The date is providentially eleven months in the future, creating the narrative window for Esther's intervention.
14: patšegen hakkᵉtāḇ lᵉhinnātēn dāt bᵉḵol-mᵉḏînāh ûmᵉḏînāh lihyôt ʿătûḏîm layyôm hazzeh: 'a copy (patšegen) of the document to be issued as law in every province, so that they would be ready for that day.' Patšegen is a Persian loanword (from Old Persian patigāma = copy/transcript), appearing only in Esther (Esther 3:14; Esther 4:8; Esther 8:13) — official administrative terminology for distributed decree copies. The advance notice — eleven months — is functionally designed to maximize participation in the killing, but providentially creates the window for Esther's counter-action. The decree's irrevocability (lihyôt ʿătûḏîm = to be prepared/ready) is the same legal permanence established in Esther 1:19 — the wall that must be navigated rather than demolished.
15: hārāṣîm yāṣᵉʾû dᵉḥûpîm biḏḇar hammelek wᵉhaddāt nittᵉnāh bᵉšûšan habîrāh wᵉhammelek wᵉhāmān yāšᵉḇû lišᵉtôt: 'the couriers went out urgently (dᵉḥûpîm) on the king's order, the decree was issued in Susa, and the king and Haman sat down to drink.' Dᵉḥûpîm (pushed, hurried) — the messengers of death dispatched with haste. The closing contrast is morally devastating: wᵉhāʿîr šûšān nāḇôḵāh, 'the city of Susa was thrown into confusion/turmoil.' Nāḇôḵāh (confused, in disarray) — even the non-Jewish population of Susa recognized the decree's wrongness, while the king and his favorite drank in indifference. The chapter ends with elite moral insulation and popular horror as the decree's two simultaneous realities — a portrait of power's capacity to generate suffering while its architects remain comfortable.
